shortDescription.text: Password stored in plaintext without hashing

fullDescription.text: The addUser function stores user passwords in plaintext. The bcrypt.hashSync call is commented out, meaning passwords are inserted directly into MongoDB without any cryptographic hashing. An attacker who gains read access to the database obtains all user credentials immediately.

shortDescription.text: Password validation accepts any 1-20 character string

fullDescription.text: The password regex /^.{1,20}$/ accepts any string of 1-20 characters with no complexity requirements. Single-character passwords, all-numeric passwords, and common dictionary words all pass validation. The commented-out fix shows the intended policy requiring uppercase, lowercase, and digits with minimum 8 characters.

shortDescription.text: Unsanitized user input passed to MongoDB findOne query

fullDescription.text: The userName parameter from req.body is passed directly to MongoDB findOne without type checking or sanitization. An attacker can submit {"userName": {"$gt": ""}} as the userName field to bypass authentication or enumerate users. Express body-parser with JSON content-type will parse nested objects from request body.

shortDescription.text: No rate limiting on signup endpoint

fullDescription.text: The POST /signup endpoint has no rate limiting middleware. An attacker can automate mass account creation or use rapid requests to enumerate existing usernames via the 'User name already in use' error message. This enables both denial-of-service through database pollution and user enumeration.

shortDescription.text: Signup reveals whether a username already exists

fullDescription.text: When a duplicate username is submitted, the signup handler returns a distinct error message 'User name already in use. Please choose another'. Combined with the lack of rate limiting and CAPTCHA, this allows automated enumeration of valid usernames in the system.

The project depends on bcrypt-nodejs version 0.0.3, which is deprecated and unmaintained. Even if the bcrypt hashing code were uncommented, this library has known issues and should be replaced with 'bcrypt' or 'bcryptjs'. Using deprecated cryptographic libraries increases risk of unpatched vulnerabilities.

shortDescription.text: Login validates passwords via plaintext string comparison

fullDescription.text: The validateLogin function compares passwords using direct string equality (fromDB === fromUser) instead of bcrypt.compareSync. This confirms passwords are stored and compared in plaintext, and the comparison is also vulnerable to timing attacks since string equality short-circuits on first differing character.

shortDescription.text: Session not regenerated on login allowing session fixation

fullDescription.text: The handleLoginRequest sets req.session.userId without calling req.session.regenerate(). The code comments explicitly document this as an intentional vulnerability. An attacker who obtains a session ID before login (e.g., via network sniffing on HTTP) can hijack the session after the victim authenticates.

message.text: Password is stored in plaintext in MongoDB. The bcrypt.hashSync call on line 542 is commented out, so the raw password from the request is inserted directly into the user document. Any database breach exposes all user credentials.

Password validation regex PASS_RE = /^.{1,20}$/ accepts any string of 1 to 20 characters. No minimum length, no complexity requirements. A password of 'a' passes validation. The commented-out fix at line 381 shows the intended stricter regex.

userName from req.body is passed unsanitized to usersCol.findOne({userName: userName}). If body-parser parses JSON, an attacker can send {"userName": {"$gt": ""}} to match any user. This affects both getUserByUserName (signup duplicate check) and validateLogin (authentication).

POST /signup has no rate limiting middleware. Route is registered at app.post('/signup', sessionHandler.handleSignup) with no throttling. Enables automated mass account creation and username enumeration.

Signup handler returns 'User name already in use. Please choose another' when a duplicate username is submitted, allowing attackers to enumerate valid usernames. Combined with absent rate limiting, automated enumeration is trivial.

Project depends on bcrypt-nodejs@0.0.3, a deprecated and unmaintained package. Even if password hashing were enabled, the underlying library may contain unpatched security issues. Replace with 'bcrypt' (native) or 'bcryptjs' (pure JS).

validateLogin compares passwords with direct string equality (fromDB === fromUser) instead of bcrypt.compareSync. Confirms passwords are stored in plaintext and comparison is vulnerable to timing side-channel attacks.

handleLoginRequest assigns req.session.userId without calling req.session.regenerate(). The session ID remains the same before and after authentication, enabling session fixation attacks. The code comments on lines 337-348 explicitly acknowledge this as a known vulnerability.
